Package Management — lock files, versioning, dependency auditing
bytes.title: Scala sbt — Lock Files, Version Pinning, and Dependency Auditing
bytes.body:
sbt has no built-in lock file — you need the `sbt-dependency-lock` plugin. Once added to `project/plugins.sbt`, run `sbt dependencyLockWrite` to generate `build.sbt.lock`, a reproducible snapshot of your full resolved dependency graph. Commit this file. In CI, `sbt dependencyLockCheck` fails the build if live resolution diverges from the lock — catching silent upgrades when upstream artifacts are republished to the same version coordinate.

Always pin exact versions in `build.sbt` (e.g., `"2.10.0"`, not `"2.+"`). Dynamic ranges like `latest.release` bypass lock semantics entirely — Ivy resolves them at build time, making builds non-reproducible. Use `%%` (double percent) to automatically append the Scala binary version suffix (`_3`, `_2.13`); this is mandatory for cross-compiled libraries.

For upgrade tracking, `sbt-updates` exposes `sbt dependencyUpdates`, listing newer coordinates against Maven Central. For CVE auditing, the OWASP `sbt-dependency-check` plugin queries the NVD database: `sbt dependencyCheck` generates an HTML report flagging known vulnerabilities across your full transitive graph — not just direct dependencies.

Commit `build.sbt.lock`, ban dynamic version ranges, and gate every PR on `dependencyLockCheck`.

bytes.topic: Performance — benchmarking, profiling, runtime characteristics per language
bytes.title: Scala JMH Benchmarking — Avoiding JVM Warm-Up Lies
bytes.body:
Naïve Scala microbenchmarks are almost always wrong. The JVM's JIT compiler aggressively optimizes hot code paths after ~10,000 invocations, meaning your first measurements are measuring interpretation overhead, not steady-state performance. Use JMH (Java Microbenchmark Harness) via the `sbt-jmh` plugin — it handles warm-up iterations, fork isolation, and dead-code elimination prevention automatically.

Key pitfalls specific to Scala: boxing of primitives in generic collections inflates allocation rates (prefer `Array[Int]` over `List[Int]` in hot paths), and lazy vals introduce a `volatile` read on every access after initialization. Use `@Benchmark` with `@BenchmarkMode(Mode.AverageTime)` and always set `@OutputTimeUnit(TimeUnit.NANOSECONDS)` for sub-microsecond work. Run with `-prof gc` to surface allocation pressure — often the real bottleneck isn't CPU but GC pauses from short-lived objects. For profiling production workloads, attach async-profiler via `-agentpath` to get flamegraphs without safepoint bias that JVM's built-in sampling introduces.

bytes.topic: Type Safety — static vs dynamic typing, type inference, generics across languages
bytes.title: Scala Type Safety — Inference, Variance, and Opaque Types
bytes.body:
Scala's type system is statically checked at compile time, but type inference — powered by its Hindley-Milner-influenced engine — means you rarely write explicit types. `val x = 42` infers `Int`; `val m = Map("a" -> 1)` infers `Map[String, Int]`.

Where Scala diverges from Java is generics with variance annotations. Declaring `class Box[+A]` makes `Box` covariant — a `Box[Dog]` is a subtype of `Box[Animal]`. Contravariance (`-A`) flips that: `Function1[-A, +B]` is contravariant in its input, so an `Animal => String` function is safely assignable to a `Dog => String` slot. This models real subtyping relationships that Java papers over with unbounded wildcards.

Type bounds tighten safety further: `[A <: Serializable]` constrains callers at compile time, not runtime. Combined with Scala 3 opaque types (`opaque type UserId = Long`), you get zero-overhead newtype wrappers — `UserId` and `Long` are identical at runtime but incompatible at compile time, eliminating entire classes of primitive-obsession bugs without any boxing cost.

bytes.topic: Runtime & Execution — compiled vs interpreted vs JIT, startup time, cold start
bytes.title: Scala on the JVM — JIT Warmup, Cold Starts, and What GraalVM Native Changes
bytes.body:
Scala compiles to JVM bytecode, not native machine code. At runtime, the JVM interprets bytecode initially, then the JIT compiler (C1 → C2 tiered compilation) profiles hot paths and emits optimized native code. This means Scala services are slow for the first few thousand requests — a real problem in serverless or short-lived container environments.

The JVM's JIT aggressively inlines, devirtualizes, and unrolls loops based on runtime profiling. Scala's heavy use of higher-order functions and trait dispatch benefits enormously from this — a `List.map` chain can be nearly as fast as a hand-written loop after warmup.

For cold-start-sensitive workloads, GraalVM Native Image compiles Scala ahead-of-time to a native binary. Startup drops from ~500ms to ~20ms. The tradeoff: no runtime JIT optimization, reflection must be configured explicitly (`native-image.properties`), and Scala's `ClassTag`/`TypeTag` machinery needs careful handling.

For long-running services (Play, Akka HTTP, http4s), JIT warmup is a non-issue. For AWS Lambda or CLI tools, reach for Native Image or consider Scala CLI with GraalVM.
bytes.code_snippet:
// build.sbt — GraalVM Native Image via sbt-native-image
enablePlugins(NativeImagePlugin)

nativeImageOptions ++= List(
  "--no-fallback",
  "--initialize-at-build-time",
  "-H:ReflectionConfigurationFiles=reflect-config.json"
)

// Measure JIT warmup impact in a tight loop
@main def benchmark(): Unit =
  val data = (1 to 1_000_000).toVector
  for i <- 1 to 5 do
    val t0 = System.nanoTime()
    val result = data.foldLeft(0L)(_ + _)
    val elapsed = (System.nanoTime() - t0) / 1_000_000
    println(s"Run $i: ${elapsed}ms, sum=$result")

bytes.topic: Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
Concurrency Model — async/await, goroutines, threads, actors — how each language handles it
bytes.title: Scala's Concurrency Model: Futures, Fibers, and Actors
bytes.body:
Scala offers three distinct concurrency models, each solving different problems. The standard library's `Future[T]` composes via `map`/`flatMap` over a thread pool driven by an implicit `ExecutionContext`, but lacks cancellation and splits error handling awkwardly between `Try` and thrown exceptions.

ZIO and Cats Effect represent the modern functional approach: lightweight fibers scheduled over a fixed-size thread pool. A ZIO fiber costs ~200 bytes vs ~1 MB for a JVM thread, supports structured concurrency via `ZIO.fork`/`fiber.join`, and encodes typed errors directly in `ZIO[R, E, A]`. Cancellation is safe and compositional — no `Thread.interrupt` footguns.

Apache Pekko (the community fork of Akka post-Lightbend licensing) implements the actor model: isolated mutable state inside actors communicates purely via message-passing. This eliminates shared-memory data races entirely and scales naturally across distributed nodes using Pekko Cluster.

Java 21 virtual threads (Project Loom) work in Scala but give up ZIO's typed error channel. Scala 3 adds no new concurrency primitives itself — the ecosystem split between effects (ZIO/Cats Effect) and actors (Pekko) is intentional and stable.
bytes.code_snippet:
import zio._

object ConcurrencyDemo extends ZIOAppDefault:
  def run =
    for
      f1 <- ZIO.attempt(fetchData("users")).fork
      f2 <- ZIO.attempt(fetchData("posts")).fork
      users <- f1.join
      posts  <- f2.join
      _      <- Console.printLine(s"Got: $users, $posts")
    yield ()

  def fetchData(endpoint: String): String = s"data-from-$endpoint"

bytes.topic: Standard Library — what's built in vs what needs a third-party library
bytes.title: Scala Standard Library — What's Built In vs What You'll Always Add
bytes.body:
Scala's stdlib covers pure computation and data structures well — but deliberately stops before I/O, serialization, and effects.

Built in: `scala.collection.immutable` provides `List`, `Vector`, `Map`, and `Set` with full functional combinators (`map`, `flatMap`, `foldLeft`). `Option`, `Try`, and `Either` handle absence and errors without exceptions. `scala.concurrent.Future` gives basic async execution. `scala.io.Source` handles file and stream reading.

Always external: There is no built-in JSON parser. Every project picks one — `circe` (codec derivation via `io.circe.generic.auto._`), `upickle` (zero-dependency, fast), or `play-json`. HTTP is absent too; `sttp` and `http4s` are the defaults. For production async, `cats-effect` or `ZIO` replace `Future` with referential transparency and structured cancellation. Configuration parsing needs `pureconfig` or `ciris`. Testing requires `ScalaTest`, `MUnit`, or `Specs2` — nothing is bundled.

The dividing line is intentional: stdlib owns immutable data and control flow; the Typelevel and ZIO ecosystems own effects, I/O, and serialization. Knowing this boundary prevents the classic mistake of stretching `Future` into production workloads where `IO` belongs.

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
Code Quality — linting, formatting, language-specific idioms
bytes.title: Scala Code Quality — Scalafmt, Scalafix, and Enforcing Idiomatic Style
bytes.body:
Scala's flexibility is a double-edged sword — without tooling enforcement, codebases drift into inconsistent, Java-flavored Scala fast. Scalafmt handles formatting; configure it with `version`, `maxColumn = 100`, and `rewrite.rules = [RedundantBraces, SortImports]` in `.scalafmt.conf`. Run `sbt scalafmtAll` in CI to fail on drift.

Scalafix goes deeper. The `DisableSyntax` rule lets you ban `null`, `var`, `throw`, and `isInstanceOf` at the compiler level — the exact constructs that signal un-idiomatic Scala. Pair it with `OrganizeImports` and `RemoveUnused` to eliminate dead code silently accumulating in long-lived services.

For idiom enforcement, `WartRemover` adds a plugin-level gate: `wartremoverWarnings ++= Warts.allBut(Wart.DefaultArguments)` catches `Any`, `asInstanceOf`, mutable collections, and `Return` statements. Prefer `Option` over nullable returns, `Either` over exceptions for expected failures, and `for`-comprehensions over nested `flatMap` chains. Use `final case class` by default — it prevents unintended subclassing and enables exhaustive pattern match checking. These aren't style preferences; they're correctness guarantees the compiler enforces for free.
bytes.code_snippet:
// project/plugins.sbt
addSbtPlugin("org.scalameta"  % "sbt-scalafmt" % "2.5.2")
addSbtPlugin("ch.epfl.scala"  % "sbt-scalafix"  % "0.12.1")
addSbtPlugin("org.wartremover" % "sbt-wartremover" % "3.1.6")

// .scalafix.conf
rules = [OrganizeImports, RemoveUnused, DisableSyntax]
DisableSyntax.noNulls = true
DisableSyntax.noVars  = true
DisableSyntax.noThrows = true

// build.sbt
wartremoverWarnings ++= Warts.allBut(
  Wart.DefaultArguments,
  Wart.Nothing
)

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — REPL, debugger, language server protocol support
bytes.title: Scala Dev Tooling — REPL, Debugger, and LSP in Your Daily Workflow
bytes.body:
The Scala REPL (`scala` or `sbt console`) is more than a scratchpad — with `:paste` mode you can drop in multi-line class definitions, and `:type` / `:kind` give you type-level introspection without compiling a full project. Ammonite (`amm`) upgrades this further: magic imports like `import $ivy.'org.typelevel::cats-core:2.10.0'` pull dependencies on the fly, and scripts are first-class.

For debugging, the JVM gives you full JDWP support. In sbt, `set fork := true` combined with `javaOptions += "-agentlib:jdwp=transport=dt_socket,server=y,suspend=n,address=5005"` exposes a debug port that IntelliJ or VS Code attaches to natively.

LSP support is handled by Metals, the de facto language server. It integrates with sbt, Mill, and Bloop via BSP. Key wins: inline type hints via `textDocument/hover`, worksheet mode (`.worksheet.sc` files evaluated inline like a REPL), and semantic highlighting. Pair Metals with Bloop for sub-second incremental compilation — `bloop compile <project>` bypasses sbt's shell overhead entirely for tighter feedback loops.

bytes.topic: Security — memory safety, injection prevention, dependency vulnerability scanning
bytes.title: Scala Security — Injection-Safe Queries with Doobie, Immutability, and sbt Vulnerability Scanning
bytes.body:
Scala's immutable-by-default collections eliminate entire categories of memory bugs — concurrent mutation, unintended aliasing, and null reference panics. Prefer `Option[A]` over null and lean on `val` + case classes to prevent shared mutable state entirely.

For injection prevention, the rule is strict: never pass user input through `Fragment.const` or string concatenation. Doobie's `sql""` interpolator lifts every interpolated value into a JDBC prepared statement parameter, making injection structurally impossible. For dynamically composed queries, use `fr""` fragments — they carry identical parameterization guarantees. Reserve `Fragment.const` exclusively for SQL identifiers (table/column names) that you fully control at compile time.

For dependency vulnerability scanning, add the sbt-dependency-check plugin to `project/plugins.sbt`. It wraps OWASP DependencyCheck and cross-references your classpath against the NVD. Run `sbt dependencyCheck` in CI with `dependencyCheckFailBuildOnCVSS := 7` to gate merges on severity. For ZIO or Cats Effect projects, audit transitive dependencies explicitly — deep effect stacks pull in many indirect libraries that Dependabot or Snyk's sbt integration can surface automatically.

bytes.topic: Scalability — horizontal scaling characteristics, thread model implications
bytes.title: Scala Horizontal Scaling — Actor Systems and Thread Model Implications
bytes.body:
Scala's horizontal scaling story is inseparable from its concurrency model. Akka's actor system uses a fixed fork-join thread pool (`akka.actor.default-dispatcher`) shared across all actors — by default sized to available CPU cores. This means a single blocking call inside an actor (e.g., a synchronous JDBC query) starves the dispatcher, causing cascading mailbox backlogs across the entire node.

For horizontal scaling, this matters because your throughput ceiling is not network or CPU — it's dispatcher saturation. The fix is isolation: route blocking operations to a dedicated dispatcher via `context.system.dispatchers.lookup("blocking-io-dispatcher")`, configured with a thread-pool-executor rather than fork-join.

With Akka Cluster, actors gain location transparency via `ActorSelection` and `ClusterSharding`. Sharding distributes entity actors across nodes deterministically by key hash, enabling stateful horizontal scaling without a shared-state bottleneck. Combined with `BackpressureStrategy` in Akka Streams, you get end-to-end flow control across a cluster — critical when scaling out consumer nodes faster than producers.

bytes.topic: Ecosystem Maturity — library quality, community size, long-term viability
bytes.title: Scala's Ecosystem Maturity — Typelevel, ZIO, and the Pekko Fork
bytes.body: Scala's ecosystem is split between two production-grade functional effect systems: Typelevel (Cats Effect 3, FS2, Http4s, Doobie) and ZIO (ZIO 2, ZIO HTTP, ZIO Quill). Both are Scala 3-native, actively maintained, and backed by large community investments. The defining ecosystem event of recent years was Lightbend moving Akka to a Business Source License — Apache Pekko emerged as the drop-in open-source fork, preserving the actor model without the licensing risk. Binary compatibility remains a real friction point: Scala publishes separate artifacts per major version (_2.13, _3), so greenfield projects should default to Scala 3 to avoid dependency version hell. The Scala Center (EPFL-backed) guarantees language longevity and drives tooling like scala-cli and Metals. Community size is smaller than Kotlin or Go, but substantially deeper — the average Scala contributor understands type systems and effect theory at a level rare in other ecosystems. For new services, commit fully to either the Typelevel or ZIO stack; cross-stack interop is possible but adds cognitive overhead that compounds at scale.

bytes.topic: Deployment — binary size, containerization, runtime dependencies
bytes.title: Scala Deployment — Shrinking Containers with sbt-native-packager and GraalVM Native Image
bytes.body:
Scala apps on the JVM carry real deployment overhead. A fat JAR via sbt-assembly bundles the Scala stdlib (~6MB) plus all transitive dependencies, yet still requires a JRE at runtime — pushing container images past 300MB by default.

Two strategies cut this dramatically. First, sbt-native-packager's DockerPlugin produces multi-stage builds out of the box. Swap the base image to `eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine` and images drop to ~180MB. Always add `-XX:+UseContainerSupport` so the JVM reads cgroup memory limits instead of host RAM, and set `-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0` to leave headroom for the OS.

Second, GraalVM Native Image via `sbt-native-image` compiles your Scala app to a self-contained binary — no JVM at runtime. Final image on `distroless/static` lands at 30–50MB. The tradeoff: reflection-heavy libraries (Akka, Play) need explicit `reflect-config.json` metadata. ZIO and Cats Effect handle native image well with `--initialize-at-build-time=zio,cats`.

Use the JVM path with Temurin Alpine for server workloads; native image for CLI tools, AWS Lambda, or anything where cold-start latency matters.
bytes.code_snippet:
// build.sbt
enablePlugins(JavaAppPackaging, DockerPlugin)

dockerBaseImage    := "eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine"
dockerExposedPorts := Seq(8080)
Docker / packageName := "myapp"

Universal / javaOptions ++= Seq(
  "-XX:+UseContainerSupport",
  "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0",
  "-Dfile.encoding=UTF-8"
)

// Strip docs and sources from the staged package
Compile / packageDoc / publishArtifact := false
Compile / packageSrc / publishArtifact := false

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
When to Choose — use cases where each language excels vs where it struggles
bytes.title: Scala — When Its Type System and Concurrency Model Work For You (and When They Fight Back)
bytes.body:
Scala shines when your domain maps naturally to algebraic data types and functional transformations. Sealed trait hierarchies with exhaustive pattern matching eliminate entire bug classes at compile time — the compiler rejects unhandled cases. For distributed data pipelines, Scala owns the space: Spark, Flink, and Kafka Streams all have first-class Scala APIs with idiomatic functional combinators.

Scala's implicit resolution and typeclass pattern enable zero-cost abstractions impossible in Java — libraries like Cats and ZIO express effect systems, resource safety, and concurrency without runtime overhead. For high-throughput async services, ZIO's fiber-based runtime outperforms thread-per-request models at scale.

Where Scala struggles: cold JVM startup makes it a poor fit for short-lived serverless functions (GraalVM native helps, but compile times worsen). The implicit/given machinery creates steep onboarding cliffs — new team members hit cryptic diverging implicit expansion errors. Build times with sbt on large projects are genuinely painful. For simple CRUD APIs where Java or Go suffice, Scala's expressiveness buys nothing and costs compile-time budget.
bytes.code_snippet:
import zio.*
import zio.http.*

// ZIO fibers handle 100k+ concurrent connections on a small thread pool
val app: HttpApp[Any] =
  Routes(
    Method.GET / "items" / int("id") -> handler { (id: Int, _: Request) =>
      ZIO
        .attemptBlocking(db.fetchItem(id))   // blocking op isolated to separate pool
        .map(item => Response.json(item.toJson))
        .catchAll(e => ZIO.succeed(Response.internalServerError(e.getMessage)))
    }
  ).toHttpApp

object Main extends ZIOAppDefault:
  def run = Server.serve(app).provide(Server.default)

bytes.topic: Language-specific — Node.js event loop, Python GIL, Go channels, Rust ownership, Elixir OTP
bytes.title: Scala Akka Typed — Behavior[T] Is Your Concurrency Primitive
bytes.body:
Akka Typed replaces Classic's untyped `receive: PartialFunction[Any, Unit]` with `Behavior[T]` — a pure function from `(ActorContext[T], T)` to the next `Behavior[T]`. This makes invalid messages a compile-time error, not a runtime `UnhandledMessage` log.

Each actor processes one message at a time from its mailbox — no shared mutable state, no locks. State transitions happen by returning a new `Behavior` (e.g., `count(n + 1)`), making actors functionally immutable even while modeling stateful systems. The type parameter `T` enforces protocol contracts at the call site: `ref ! WrongMessage` simply won't compile.

Where Go uses channels for CSP-style concurrency and Elixir uses OTP GenServers backed by the BEAM scheduler, Scala uses `Behavior[T]` hierarchies supervised via `Behaviors.supervise(...).onFailure[SomeException](SupervisorStrategy.restart)` — declarative, composable, no subclassing required.

`ActorContext` exposes `spawn`, `watch`, and `scheduleOnce` without breaking purity. For purely functional stacks, ZIO's `Hub`/`Queue` or Cats Effect's `Ref` + `Channel` deliver the same isolation without the Akka runtime, but Akka's location transparency and clustering remain unmatched for distributed state.
bytes.code_snippet:
import akka.actor.typed.{ActorSystem, Behavior, ActorRef}
import akka.actor.typed.scaladsl.Behaviors

object Counter:
  sealed trait Cmd
  case object Inc                        extends Cmd
  case class Get(replyTo: ActorRef[Int]) extends Cmd

  def apply(): Behavior[Cmd] = active(0)

  private def active(n: Int): Behavior[Cmd] =
    Behaviors.receive { (_, msg) =>
      msg match
        case Inc            => active(n + 1)
        case Get(replyTo)   => replyTo ! n; Behaviors.same
    }

@main def run(): Unit =
  val system = ActorSystem(Counter(), "counter")
  system ! Counter.Inc
  system ! Counter.Inc

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
bytes.title: Scala Error Handling — Try, Either, and Typed Error Propagation
bytes.body:
Scala's idiomatic error handling favors algebraic types over exceptions. `Try[A]` wraps exception-throwing JVM code into `Success[A]` or `Failure(Throwable)` — useful at boundaries with Java libraries. But `Either[E, A]` is the domain workhorse: `Left[E]` carries a typed error, `Right[A]` carries the result. For-comprehensions chain `Either` values and short-circuit on the first `Left` automatically — no nested try/catch.

Convert JVM exceptions to typed errors at the boundary: `Try(riskyCall()).toEither.left.map(AppError.fromThrowable)`. For validation where you need to accumulate multiple errors rather than short-circuit, reach for `cats.data.Validated[NonEmptyList[E], A]` — its `mapN` collects all failures. ZIO encodes errors directly in the type signature as `ZIO[R, E, A]`, enabling typed interruption and fiber-safe error channels.

Avoid `throw` in domain or library code — callers cannot statically see what you throw. Reserve exceptions for unrecoverable states like `OutOfMemoryError`. Define a `sealed trait AppError` hierarchy and return `Either[AppError, A]` from service layers; exhaustive pattern matching at the API boundary ensures no error is silently swallowed.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
bytes.title: Scala Testing — ScalaTest, MUnit, and the Pure-Function Philosophy
bytes.body:
Scala's two dominant test frameworks are ScalaTest and MUnit. ScalaTest offers multiple styles — AnyFunSuite for assertion-based tests, AnyFlatSpec for BDD-style specs, and AnyWordSpec for nested describe blocks. MUnit, from Scalameta, is leaner: just test("name") {} blocks with precise diff output via clue() and first-class scala-cli support.

The idiomatic Scala philosophy is to push side effects to the edges and test pure functions exhaustively. A method returning Either[AppError, User] needs no mocks — just construct inputs and assert outputs. Reserve ScalaMock or mockito-scala for IO boundaries: repositories, HTTP clients, and message queues.

Run tests with sbt test (all suites) or sbt testOnly com.example.UserServiceSpec for a single suite. For MUnit via scala-cli: scala-cli test . --dep org.scalameta::munit::1.0.0.

For property-based testing, ScalaCheck integrates with both frameworks — via Checkers in ScalaTest or munit-scalacheck. Define generators with Gen.alphaStr, Gen.posNum[Int], or custom Arbitrary instances to cover edge cases that hand-written examples miss. Properties like forAll { (s: String) => decode(encode(s)) == s } are far more resilient than example-based tests alone.
bytes.code_snippet:
import org.scalatest.funsuite.AnyFunSuite

class ParseAmountSuite extends AnyFunSuite:
  def parseAmount(s: String): Either[String, BigDecimal] =
    s.toBigDecimalOption.toRight(s"Invalid amount: $s")

  test("valid decimal returns Right"):
    assert(parseAmount("9.99") == Right(BigDecimal("9.99")))

  test("non-numeric input returns Left"):
    assert(parseAmount("abc").isLeft)

  test("negative value is parsed correctly"):
    assert(parseAmount("-1.50") == Right(BigDecimal("-1.50")))

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
bytes.title: Scala Memory Management — JVM GC, Boxing Traps, and Zero-Cost Wrappers
bytes.body:
Scala runs on the JVM — all memory management is GC-driven, not ownership-based. The heap holds every object instance: case classes, closures, collections. Primitives (Int, Long, Double) live on the stack or in registers when the JIT's escape analysis confirms they don't outlive their scope.

The biggest Scala-specific trap is boxing: generic containers like List[Int] silently convert primitives to java.lang.Integer heap objects. In hot paths, prefer Array[Int] — the JVM stores these as unboxed int[]. For generic classes, annotate with @specialized to generate primitive-specific bytecode and eliminate boxing entirely.

Opaque types (Scala 3) and value classes (extends AnyVal) let you model domain types like UserId or Millis with zero heap overhead — the wrapper is erased at bytecode level.

Memory safety in Scala comes from immutability: val bindings, immutable List/Map/Set by default, and case class copy semantics prevent aliased mutation bugs. No pointer arithmetic means no dangling references — but GC leaks still occur when long-lived structures retain closures capturing large object graphs. Common culprit: a var cache: Map[K, V] in a singleton that grows unbounded without eviction.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
bytes.title: Scala Production Monitoring — Kamon Metrics, G1GC Pauses, and async-profiler
bytes.body:
Kamon is the standard Scala metrics library — it integrates with Akka, Pekko, Tapir, and http4s via auto-instrumentation modules. The `kamon-system-metrics` module scrapes JVM heap, GC pause duration, and thread counts via JMX, then ships them to Prometheus or Datadog with zero manual instrumentation.

Enable G1GC logging at startup: `-XX:+UseG1GC -Xlog:gc*:file=/var/log/gc.log:time,uptime`. G1 mixed-collection pauses above 200ms typically signal humongous object allocations — in Scala, these arise from large `Vector`, `HashMap`, or case class construction inside hot paths. Raise the threshold with `-XX:G1HeapRegionSize=16m`.

For CPU and allocation profiling on live production JVMs, async-profiler is the standard tool — it uses `AsyncGetCallTrace` instead of safepoint sampling, giving accurate call stacks even under load. Attach without restarting the process:

```bash
./asprof -e cpu -d 30 -f /tmp/profile.html $(pgrep -f 'scala.main')
```

For Akka-based systems, `kamon-akka` adds mailbox depth, actor message latency, and dead-letter counts — signals invisible to JVM-level metrics but critical for diagnosing backpressure and supervision failures.
